title: Types of Family Structure
examTip: Always name the sociologist and date when describing a perspective — 'Murdock (1949) argued…' scores more marks than just stating an idea.
introTips: AQA loves asking you to 'describe one type of family' — learn at least 4 types with a statistic each.
Know the difference between 'classic extended' (same household) and 'modified extended' (geographically separate but emotionally close).
Be ready to evaluate family types from at least two different sociological perspectives.
intro: Why does it matter how we define a 'family'? Because the definition shapes laws, welfare benefits, housing policy, and who gets recognised and who gets left out. Sociologists disagree sharply about which family forms are 'normal', 'ideal', or 'declining' — and those disagreements have real consequences for real people. In this lesson you'll map the full range of family structures found in the UK today and learn what different sociological perspectives say about them.

keyPoints.heading: Types of family structure
keyPoints.content: A nuclear family consists of a married or cohabiting couple and their dependent children living together. George Peter Murdock (1949) argued it was universal — found in all 250 societies he studied. Historically the most common UK household type. An extended family adds other relatives — either sharing one household (classic extended) or living separately but maintaining close contact (modified extended, Litwak 1960). More common in South Asian British communities and in LICs globally. A lone-parent family consists of one parent (usually female) and dependent children — now around 25% of UK families with dependent children, created through divorce, separation, bereavement, or choice. A reconstituted (blended or step) family forms when partners bring children from previous relationships — roughly 10–15% of UK families with dependent children, growing as divorce and remarriage increase. A same-sex family has two parents of the same sex; Civil Partnership Act 2004 and Marriage (Same Sex Couples) Act 2014 gave legal recognition. A cohabiting family is an unmarried couple living together with children — cohabitation is now the norm before marriage.
writeBullets: Nuclear family — married/cohabiting couple + dependent children in one household (Murdock, 1949: universal)
Classic extended family — nuclear + other relatives sharing one household
Modified extended family — geographically separate relatives in regular close contact (Litwak, 1960)
Lone-parent family — one parent + children; ~25% of UK families; majority female-headed
Reconstituted/blended family — partners with children from previous relationships; ~10–15% UK
Same-sex family — two same-sex parents + children; Marriage Equality Act 2014
keyPoints.memTip: NELSON = Nuclear, Extended, Lone-parent, Same-sex, Offered (cohabiting), Nurtured (reconstituted) — six types covered.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget 'modified extended' — it's a favourite AQA distinction.

keyPoints.heading: Sociological perspectives on family types
keyPoints.content: Murdock (1949, Functionalist) surveyed 250 societies and concluded the nuclear family is universal, performing four essential functions: sexual, reproductive, economic, and educational. Parsons (1955, Functionalist) argued the nuclear family is 'functionally fit' for industrial society — it is geographically mobile and not burdened by extended kin obligations (functional fit thesis). The New Right (Murray, 1984; Morgan, 1999) sees the nuclear family with a male breadwinner and female carer as the 'gold standard'; other forms are viewed as 'deficient' or socially damaging — especially father-absent lone-parent families. Feminist perspectives (Oakley, Millett) reject idealisation of the nuclear family as a site of women's oppression and inequality. Postmodernist perspectives (Stacey, 1990) argue 'the family' is a myth — in reality there are just 'families'; diversity of family forms should be celebrated as individual freedom.
writeBullets: Murdock (1949) — nuclear family universal; performs sexual, reproductive, economic, educational functions
Parsons (1955) — 'functional fit': nuclear family suits industrial society (mobile, specialised)
New Right (Murray, 1984) — nuclear family is the ideal; lone-parent families create social problems
Feminist view — nuclear family oppresses women; domestic labour unequal
Postmodernist view (Stacey, 1990) — celebrate family diversity; no single 'normal' form
keyPoints.memTip: Four perspectives: Function → New Right → Feminist → Post (FNFP). Each either supports or criticises the nuclear norm.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions often ask students to 'evaluate' the nuclear family — knowing two or three perspectives makes this straightforward.

keyPoints.heading: Trends and statistics
keyPoints.content: UK family structures have shifted dramatically since the 1970s. Lone-parent families rose from 8% (1972) to 25% (2023) of families with dependent children. Cohabiting couples numbered 3.6 million in the UK in 2021 — the fastest-growing family type. Same-sex couple families: 211,000 counted in the 2021 Census. Average household size has declined from 3.0 (1960) to 2.4 people. Marriage rates have fallen sharply — from approximately 400,000 marriages per year in 1970 to 221,000 in 2019. The Divorce Reform Act 1969 (irretrievable breakdown sufficient grounds) triggered a steep rise in divorce rates; no-fault divorce introduced in England and Wales in 2022. Reconstituted families account for around 10% of families with dependent children. These trends reflect wider social changes: secularisation, women's financial independence, changing gender norms, and postmodern individualism.
writeBullets: Lone-parent families: 8% (1972) → 25% (2023) of families with dependent children
Cohabiting couples: 3.6m in UK — fastest-growing household type
Same-sex couple families: 211,000 (2021 Census)
Average household size: 3.0 (1960) → 2.4 people now
Marriages per year: ~400,000 (1970) → 221,000 (2019) — historic low
Divorce Reform Act 1969 — irretrievable breakdown sufficient grounds; no-fault divorce 2022
keyPoints.memTip: Think of the '4-to-2' trend: household size, marriages, extended families — all shrinking. Lone-parent and cohabiting — rising.
keyPoints.tutor_note: A question asking you to 'describe one trend in family life' just needs one statistic — pick the clearest one you know.

keyPoints.heading: The 'cereal packet family' and media ideals
keyPoints.content: Edmund Leach (1967) coined the phrase 'cereal packet family' to describe how the heterosexual nuclear family — mum, dad, two children — is constantly presented as 'normal' in advertising, media, and politics. Leach argued this idealised image damages people who don't conform to it. The 'conventional family' has always been a largely white, middle-class ideal — working-class and ethnic minority families have historically had very different household arrangements. New Right thinkers see departures from the nuclear norm as social problems requiring policy correction. Postmodernists (Stacey, Beck) celebrate diversity and argue individuals now have the freedom to construct their own family arrangements. Sociologists call this familial ideology — the powerful set of ideas promoting one family form as natural and universal, which can marginalise and stigmatise alternative family forms.
writeBullets: Cereal packet family (Leach, 1967) — nuclear family idealised in advertising and media
Familial ideology — promotes nuclear family as natural and normal; stigmatises other forms
New Right — departures from nuclear norm are social problems; policy should promote traditional family
Postmodernist — celebrate diversity; individuals freely construct own family arrangements
Working-class and ethnic minority families have always had more diverse arrangements
keyPoints.memTip: Leach's cereal packet = the image on the box is perfect but bears little relation to reality inside.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The concept of 'familial ideology' is useful for evaluation — media images shape expectations and can cause harm.

tables.title: Family types: definition, UK statistics, and sociological assessment
rows: Historically most common; declining as % of households
Functionalists: ideal form. Feminists: site of oppression. New Right: gold standard
Less common in UK; more common in South Asian communities
Functionalists: pre-industrial norm. Some see it as supportive network
Relatives geographically separate but in close contact
Very widespread — most families maintain kin ties
Litwak (1960): modern families still emotionally extended
New Right: socially damaging. Feminists: often result of women escaping unhappy marriages
Partners with children from previous relationships
Postmodernists: reflects modern diversity and personal choice
Civil Partnership 2004, Marriage Equality 2014. Growing social acceptance
Postmodernists: reflects individual freedom. New Right: less stable than marriage

0.question: Which sociologist argued in 1949 that the nuclear family is universal, found in all 250 societies studied?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Murdock (1949) studied 250 societies and concluded the nuclear family is universal and performs four essential functions.
0.wrongFeedback: Not quite. It was Murdock (1949) who made this claim. Parsons (1955) built on it with the 'functional fit' thesis. Leach coined 'cereal packet family'. Murray is a New Right thinker.

2.question: What does the term 'modified extended family' (Litwak, 1960) mean?
options: Relatives who live separately but maintain regular close contact
All generations of a family living in one large household
A nuclear family with step-children from previous relationships
An unmarried couple living together with children
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The 'modified extended' family is geographically dispersed but emotionally and practically close — the most common arrangement in modern UK.
2.wrongFeedback: That describes the 'classic extended' family. The 'modified extended' (Litwak, 1960) is the more modern form — relatives live apart but stay in close regular contact.

3.question: Edmund Leach (1967) coined the phrase 'cereal packet family'. What did he mean by this?
options: The nuclear family is constantly presented as normal in advertising and media, despite being an idealised image
Families have become more focused on consuming breakfast products
The nuclear family performs four essential functions for society
Working-class families eat less nutritious food than middle-class families
3.correctFeedback: Spot on. Leach argued the nuclear family ideal — as seen on cereal boxes — is a powerful but damaging image that marginalises those who don't conform.
3.wrongFeedback: The cereal packet family refers to how advertising and media constantly portray the nuclear family as the normal, ideal family form — Leach argued this is damaging to those who don't fit.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'classic extended' (all in one household) with 'modified extended' (geographically separate but emotionally close) — these are different concepts.
Saying all ethnic minority families are extended — this is a stereotype; family structures vary enormously within every ethnic group.
Assuming the nuclear family has always been dominant — it was actually less common historically than we might think, especially among the working class.
Attributing the 'cereal packet family' idea to Murdock — it was Leach (1967), not Murdock.
Forgetting to include statistics when describing trends — AQA mark schemes reward precise figures (e.g., 25% lone-parent, 3.6m cohabiting).
examTips: When asked to 'describe one type of family structure', name it, define it, and add a UK statistic — three elements for three marks.
For evaluation questions, always use named sociologists with dates: 'Murdock (1949) argued… however, Stacey (1990) challenged this by…'
Don't just list perspectives — apply them. Say what Functionalists OR Feminists OR New Right SPECIFICALLY say about the family type in the question.
In extended writing, structure your argument: point → evidence (sociologist + date) → explain → evaluate/counter-point.
revisionCardBullets: Nuclear family: couple + dependent children — Murdock (1949) called it universal
Extended (classic): all relatives in one household; (modified): separate but close contact — Litwak (1960)
Lone-parent: ~25% of UK families (2023); mostly female-headed
Reconstituted/blended: partners with children from previous relationships; ~10–15% UK
Same-sex families: 211,000 (2021 Census); Marriage Equality Act 2014
Parsons (1955): 'functional fit' — nuclear family suits industrial society
New Right (Murray, 1984): nuclear family is the ideal; other forms are socially damaging
Feminist view: nuclear family is a site of women's oppression
Postmodern view (Stacey, 1990): celebrate family diversity; no one 'normal' form
Cereal packet family (Leach, 1967): nuclear family idealised in media; harmful to those who don't conform
Familial ideology: set of ideas promoting nuclear family as natural and universal
